tossups.question: This poet declares “You have given me the shell, Satan” in a poem whose beach imagery includes a sun “exploded in the sea” and a “tarantula rattling at the lily’s foot.” This poet wrote “I think the sea has thrown itself upon me and been answered” in a letter to Waldo Frank. This poet included a revised version of “O Carib Isle” in an “island sheaf” of poems named for Key West. He wrote of a “scattered chapter, livid hieroglyph” given back by the (*) “calyx of death’s bounty” in a poem that states “High in the azure steeps / Monody shall not wake the mariner.” This poet described the ocean as a “great wink of eternity” in a sequence of six love poems for the sailor Emil Opffer. This poet, who jumped into the Gulf of Mexico to commit suicide, included “Voyages” and “At Melville’s Tomb” in his collection White Buildings. For 10 points, name this poet of The Bridge.
tossups.question_sanitized: This poet declares "You have given me the shell, Satan" in a poem whose beach imagery includes a sun "exploded in the sea" and a "tarantula rattling at the lily's foot." This poet wrote "I think the sea has thrown itself upon me and been answered" in a letter to Waldo Frank. This poet included a revised version of "O Carib Isle" in an "island sheaf" of poems named for Key West. He wrote of a "scattered chapter, livid hieroglyph" given back by the (*) "calyx of death's bounty" in a poem that states "High in the azure steeps / Monody shall not wake the mariner." This poet described the ocean as a "great wink of eternity" in a sequence of six love poems for the sailor Emil Opffer. This poet, who jumped into the Gulf of Mexico to commit suicide, included "Voyages" and "At Melville's Tomb" in his collection White Buildings. For 10 points, name this poet of The Bridge.

tossups.question: A fragment by this poet reading “I have seen my ghost broken / My body blessed / And Eden / Scraped from my mother’s breast” appeared in the final issue of the magazine Antaeus. Charles Wright claimed to be “making a language where nothing stays” in a poem titled for this author, who influenced D.A. Powell and Clayton Eshleman. Melvin Tolson’s Libretto for the Republic of Liberia is modeled on a long poem by this author, whose friend Allen Tate introduced his debut collection. Phillip Levine opened his collection The Simple Truth with a poem about this American’s meeting with (*) García Lorca. This poet wrote “The dice of drowned men’s bones he saw bequeath / An embassy” in a poem set at the resting place of one of his 19th-century gay influences. For 10 points, name this author of the collection White Buildings and “At Melville’s Tomb.”
tossups.question_sanitized: A fragment by this poet reading "I have seen my ghost broken / My body blessed / And Eden / Scraped from my mother's breast" appeared in the final issue of the magazine Antaeus. Charles Wright claimed to be "making a language where nothing stays" in a poem titled for this author, who influenced D.A. Powell and Clayton Eshleman. Melvin Tolson's Libretto for the Republic of Liberia is modeled on a long poem by this author, whose friend Allen Tate introduced his debut collection. Phillip Levine opened his collection The Simple Truth with a poem about this American's meeting with (*) Garcia Lorca. This poet wrote "The dice of drowned men's bones he saw bequeath / An embassy" in a poem set at the resting place of one of his 19th-century gay influences. For 10 points, name this author of the collection White Buildings and "At Melville's Tomb."
tossups.answer: Hart Crane (Wright’s poem is “Portrait of the Artist with Hart Crane”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Hart Crane (Wright's poem is "Portrait of the Artist with Hart Crane")

tossups.question: This event titles a prose poem by Mark Ford in which the speaker attends a party consisting entirely of men in their seventies all named Harold. An essay that ends by considering Al Alvarez’s The Savage God opens with images of this event in which a participant shed “his bathrobe frugally” and “went sudden as a springboard,” in contrast to a woman who would awkwardly lift the many layers of her bedding to pee. This real-life event is connected to the “doctrine of Emerson and Whitman” in the final essay in Yvor Winter’s book In Defense of Reason. William H. Gass juxtaposed this event with the slow, spiteful death of his mother in the opening of his essay “The (*) Doomed in Their Sinking.” This event occurred shortly after departing Vera Cruz with Peggy Cowley and happened after its subject was attacked for making sexual advances toward crew members of the Orizaba. For 10 points, what 1932 event that took place at the side of a ship in the Gulf of Mexico involved the author of “The Broken Tower” and The Bridge?

This real-life event is connected to the "doctrine of Emerson and Whitman" in the final essay in Yvor Winter's book In Defense of Reason. William H. Gass juxtaposed this event with the slow, spiteful death of his mother in the opening of his essay "The (*) Doomed in Their Sinking." This event occurred shortly after departing Vera Cruz with Peggy Cowley and happened after its subject was attacked for making sexual advances toward crew members of the Orizaba. For 10 points, what 1932 event that took place at the side of a ship in the Gulf of Mexico involved the author of "The Broken Tower" and The Bridge?
tossups.answer: the suicide of Hart Crane [accept death or drowning in place of suicide; prompt on answers missing Crane by asking “of what author?”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: the suicide of Hart Crane [accept death or drowning in place of suicide; prompt on answers missing Crane by asking "of what author?"]

tossups.question: A poem titled for this author notes, “Frosted eyes there were that lifted altars; / And silent answers crept across the stars.” The title poem of a collection titled for a character created by this author describes “Executioners near the woman at the window” and how “in the heart’s veined temple, all statues have been smashed”. That poem quotes an Emily Dickinson poem and begins with an epigraph by Laurence Hope. A character created by this author takes his name from Marvell’s “Upon Appleton House” and appears in a novel containing a poem in which the “oozy (*) weeds … twist” about the “sleepy” title character. Characters created by this author title an Agha Shahid Ali collection and a poem in which the title character is “in the Darbies”. For 10 points, what novelist described “The ribs and terrors in the whale” in his magnum opus Moby Dick?
tossups.question_sanitized: A poem titled for this author notes, "Frosted eyes there were that lifted altars; / And silent answers crept across the stars." The title poem of a collection titled for a character created by this author describes "Executioners near the woman at the window" and how "in the heart's veined temple, all statues have been smashed". That poem quotes an Emily Dickinson poem and begins with an epigraph by Laurence Hope. A character created by this author takes his name from Marvell's "Upon Appleton House" and appears in a novel containing a poem in which the "oozy (*) weeds ... twist" about the "sleepy" title character. Characters created by this author title an Agha Shahid Ali collection and a poem in which the title character is "in the Darbies". For 10 points, what novelist described "The ribs and terrors in the whale" in his magnum opus Moby Dick?
tossups.answer: Herman Melville [accept “At Melville’s Tomb”] (“At Melville’s Tomb” is by Hart Crane.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Herman Melville [accept "At Melville's Tomb"] ("At Melville's Tomb" is by Hart Crane.)

tossups.question: The title subject of a poem by this author can "condense eternity," as "we have seen night lifted in thine arms." A poem by this author describes how "Rip forgot the office hours, / and he forgot the pay; / Van Winkle sweeps a tenement / way down on Avenue A." This author wrote a dramatic monologue in which Columbus thanks the Virgin Mary for safe passage. A poem by this author ends with the plea "of the curveship lend a myth to God." This author wrote the invocation "O Appalachian Spring!" in the section "Powhatan's Daughter." Two years before he drunkenly jumped from a ship into the Gulf of Mexico, this author published a collection including a "proem" partly titled for Brooklyn. For 10 points, name this modernist poet of The Bridge.

Two years before he drunkenly jumped from a ship into the Gulf of Mexico, this author published a collection including a "proem" partly titled for Brooklyn. For 10 points, name this modernist poet of The Bridge.

tossups.question: After a character in a poem by an author with this surname shouts, “Ah, there is no God,” a “sword from the sky” smites him dead. One poem by an author with this surname begins, “There are no stars tonight / but those of memory.” An author with this surname described “a famished kitten on the step” in a poem beginning, “We make our meek adjustments.” An author with this surname described the (*) “clang and clang of spear and shield” made by the title group who “came from the sea.” “My Grandmother’s Love Letters” is a poem by an author with this surname who included the sections “Cape Hatteras” and “Powhatan’s Daughter” in a long poem that begins with a “Proem.” For 10 points, give this surname shared by the American poets of The Black Riders and The Bridge, Stephen and Hart.

An author with this surname described the (*) "clang and clang of spear and shield" made by the title group who "came from the sea." "My Grandmother's Love Letters" is a poem by an author with this surname who included the sections "Cape Hatteras" and "Powhatan's Daughter" in a long poem that begins with a "Proem." For 10 points, give this surname shared by the American poets of The Black Riders and The Bridge, Stephen and Hart.
tossups.answer: Crane [accept Stephen Crane; accept Hart Crane or Harold Hart Crane]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Crane [accept Stephen Crane; accept Hart Crane or Harold Hart Crane]

tossups.question: A poem titled for a landmark in this place declares “I am / the perfect man / the Buddha of this World.” A poem that opens “How many dawns, chill from his rippling rest / The seagull’s wings shall dip and pivot him” and asks a landmark in this place to “sometime sweep, descend / And of the curveship lend a myth to God.” This place titles a poem that describes the “similitudes of the past and those of the future” and asks (*) “what is the count of the scores or hundreds of years between us?” A poem titled for this place describes “crowds of men and women attired in the usual costumes” and declares “you furnish your parts towards the soul.” A landmark in this place inspired a modernist epic by Hart Crane. For ten points, Walt Whitman declared “flood-tide below me! I watch you face to face” in a poem titled for a ferry trip to what place?
tossups.answer: Brooklyn [accept the Brooklyn Bridge] (The clued poems are Jack Keroac’s “Brooklyn Bridge Blues,” Hart Crane’s “Proem: To Brooklyn Bridge;” and Whitman’s “Crossing Brooklyn Ferry.”)

tossups.question: In a poem by this author, a man who sold kitchenware in the Yucatán rambles about the "birdless mouth” of the Popocatépetl (“POPE-a-CAT-a-petal”) volcano as the song "Stamboul Rose" plays. A religious ceremony this author witnessed in Tepoztlán (“TEH-pust-LAHN”) inspired the setting of one of his poems, whose speaker hears “terraced echoes prostrate on the plain” while wandering on a lawn. The speaker imagines finding a “famished kitten on the step” in a poem by this author that opens, “We (*) make our meek adjustments.” This poet had his only heterosexual affair with Peggy Baird in Mexico, where he wrote about the church bells of the title “Broken Tower” shortly before committing suicide in its Gulf. For 10 points, name this poet of “Chaplinesque” who included sections like “Cutty Sark” in his epic The Bridge.

tossups.question: An early study of this poet's longest work by L. S. Dembo is titled for this author's "Sanskrit Charge." A poem by this author recalls "the songs that gypsies dealt us at Marseille" after observing that "imponderable the dinosaur sinks slow." This poet notes that "I think of cinemas, panoramic sleights," and describes seeing an "inviolate curve...till elevators drop us from our day" in a poem that describes a seagull's wings "building high over the chained bay waters Liberty." Christopher Columbus delivers a monologue in this author's poem "Ave (AH-vay) Maria." This author aimed to provide a more optimistic counterpart to T. S. Eliot's The Waste Land in a book-length poem whose sections include a "Proem," "Powhatan's Daughter," and "Cape Hatteras." For 10 points, name this modernist poet who depicted a Brooklyn landmark in The Bridge.

tossups.question: A poem by this author, which opens ‘Invariably when wine redeems the sight’, was ruinously edited by Marianne Moore and retitled to “Again”. This poet described ‘the logic of metaphor’ in a letter to Harriet Monroe after she rejected a poem by them that states how ‘silent answers crept across the stars’. That poem by this poet, which states ‘the dice of drowned men’s bones… bequeath an embassy’, depicts another author’s (*) grave. This poet asked ‘how many dawns… The seagull’s wings shall dip and pivot him’ in the first part of a poem aimed to provide an uplifting response to The Waste Land. That poem includes sections like a “Proem” and “Cape Hatteras”, and is centred on a New York landmark. For 10 points, name this modernist American poet of “At Melville’s Tomb” and The Bridge.

tossups.question: Edmund Wilson wrote that this author had “something like a great style, if there could be such a thing as a great style which was … not … applied to any subject at all.” This author wrote about the “inevitable thumb” that “slowly chafes its puckered index toward us” and “a kitten in the wilderness” in one poem. This author criticized The Waste Land as “so damned dead,” and attempted to write an epic positive counterpart to it. This poet of (*) “Chaplinesque” described his lover’s “argosy of bright hair” in a cycle of poems dedicated to Emil Opffer. He wrote about bells that “swing I know not where” and “the matrix of the heart” in his last poem, “The Broken Tower.” This poet described a seagull “Over the chained bay waters Liberty” in a ‘proem’ that opens his magnum opus. For 10 points, name this author of Voyages, “At Melville’s Tomb,” and The Bridge.

tossups.question: This poet wrote “these fine collapses are not lies / More than the pirouettes of any pliant cane” in one poem. This poet of “Chaplainesque” wrote “Compass, quadrant and sextant contrive/ No farther tides ... High in the azure steeps / Monody shall not wake the mariner” in a poem set at another writer’s tomb. In one another work, this poet addressed a structure as “O (*) Sleepless as the river under thee / Vaulting the sea, the prairies’ dreaming sod.”; that work by this poet of “At Melville’s Tomb” includes the sections “Powhatan's Daughter” and “Cape Hatteras.” For 10 points, name this author of the collection White Buildings and epic poem The Bridge?

tossups.question: A negative review of this author’s work by William Logan triggered a nasty letter by his biographer Paul Mariani that disputed what kind of jacket this poet was wearing when he died. He described “fine collapses” that “are not lies / More than the pirouettes of any pliant cane” in a poem that begins: “We make our meek adjustments, / Contented with such random consolations / As the wind deposits.” The line “Permit me voyage, love, into your hands” appears in a poem by him that describes “Bright striped urchins flay[ing] each other with sand.” This poet of “Chaplinesque” and “Voyages” described Columbus’s discovery of America in “Ave Maria,” which joins “Powhatan’s Daughter” as sections of his longest poem. For 10 points, name this American poet of White Buildings and The Bridge.

tossups.question: In one poem, this writer cautioned that “steps must be gentle” in a space where “it is all hung by an invisible white hair” that “trembles as birch limbs webbing the air.” The speaker of that poem predicts that he would stumble as he leads the title figure “by the hand through much of what she would not understand.” This poet wrote that “silent answers crept across the stars” in a work that claims that “compass, quadrant and sextant contrive no farther tides.” The first section of his best-known work asks the addressee to “descend and of the curveship lend a myth to God.” In a poem that opens, “There are no (*) stars tonight but those of memory,” this poet wrote about objects pressed “into a corner of the roof” that belonged to his “mother’s mother.” In another poem this author of “My Grandmother’s Love Letters” wrote, “this fabulous shadow only the sea keeps,” about the grave of another writer. For 10 points, name this poet of “At Melville’s Tomb” and a long poem entitled The Bridge.

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: As part of the Catalan Renaissance, Catalan poets could win the title "Master of the Gay Science" in a 19th-century revival of Occitan "games" named for these objects. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these objects. A 19th-century poetry collection titled for these objects had six “condemned poems” censored from their publication, though they were later published in a separate volume titled Scraps.

Jocs florals winner Jacint Verdaguer wrote a long poem about this person hearing the story of the title empire. A 1930 dramatic monologue ends with this person contemplating “kingdoms naked in the trembling heart” and a “Hand of Fire.”
The original Jocs florals were created to revive the poetry of these Occitan lyric poets, who often wrote about courtly love and included Arnaut Daniel.
parts_sanitized: Name these objects. A 19th-century poetry collection titled for these objects had six "condemned poems" censored from their publication, though they were later published in a separate volume titled Scraps.

Jocs florals winner Jacint Verdaguer wrote a long poem about this person hearing the story of the title empire. A 1930 dramatic monologue ends with this person contemplating "kingdoms naked in the trembling heart" and a "Hand of Fire."
The original Jocs florals were created to revive the poetry of these Occitan lyric poets, who often wrote about courtly love and included Arnaut Daniel.
answers: flowers [accept Les Fleurs du mal; accept floral games or Jocs florals; reject specific types of flowers]
Christopher Columbus (The poem is L’Atlàntida. The monologue is Hart Crane’s “Ave Maria.”)
answers_sanitized: flowers [accept Les Fleurs du mal; accept floral games or Jocs florals; reject specific types of flowers]
Christopher Columbus (The poem is L'Atlantida. The monologue is Hart Crane's "Ave Maria.")

bonuses.leadin: A poem about this person asks, “Did her eyes slant in the old way?” in its section “Eidolon (“AI-doh-lon”)”. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this mythological woman. A poem by H.D. titled for this woman “in Egypt” was based on a feminist reading of a Euripides play in which she is abducted by Paris for her beauty, inciting the Trojan War.
H.D. wrote Helen in Egypt as a response to this unfinished long poem, which rails against “usura” and was largely written while its author was imprisoned on treason charges in Italy.

This other modernist poet imagined Helen’s marriage to Faustus in a poem inspired by his affair with Emil Opffer, who also influenced an erotic six-part poem by this author that describes “bright striped urchins.”
answers: Helen of Troy [or Helénē or Helena; accept Helen in Egypt]
Hart Crane [or Harold Hart Crane] (The other poem is “Voyages.”)

packet.name: 03 - Cornell A, CWRU A, Harvard A, Iowa A

answers_sanitized: Helen of Troy [or Helene or Helena; accept Helen in Egypt]
The Cantos [accept The Pisan Cantos] (by Ezra Pound)
Hart Crane [or Harold Hart Crane] (The other poem is "Voyages.")

bonuses.leadin: A poem by this author opens, “There are no stars tonight / But those of memory.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who wrote about his grandmother Elizabeth’s “love letters” in that poem. This poet also described how “the wind deposits / In slithered and too ample pockets” in “Chaplinesque.”

The speaker of a poem by this man describes writing his abusive, illiterate father’s love letters for him. This poet half expects to see his own name “in letters like smoke” among a list of names in a poem depicting a visit to the Vietnam Veterans Memorial.
This author’s “Letter to Her Husband, Absent upon Publick Employment” follows a poem dedicated to her “dear and loving husband” in a posthumous edition of The Tenth Muse Lately Sprung Up in America.
answers: Yusef Komunyakaa [prompt on James William Brown] (The first poem is “My Father’s Love Letters”; the second is “Facing It.”)

bonuses.leadin: Name some poets who wrote about Charlie Chaplin, for 10 points each.
parts: This poet wrote about how “we make our meek adjustments” in the first line of his poem “Chaplinesque” from the collection White Buildings.
This author of the poem “Chaplin Upon the Cliff, Dining Alone” wrote a short story in which a man dreams that he’s in a theater watching a movie about his parents’ courtship.
The image of “your mother” watching “Chaplin dance in youth” appears in “Kaddish,” a poem by this Beat poet who wrote “Howl.”
answers: Delmore Schwartz (That story is “In Dreams Begin Responsibilities.”)

packet.name: C (MSU A, NYU B, UCLA A, and Virginia A)

answers_sanitized: Delmore Schwartz (That story is "In Dreams Begin Responsibilities.")

bonuses.leadin: The “Proem” to this text describes how “A bedlamite speeds to thy parapets, tilting there momently, shrill shirt ballooning,” and begins by asking “How many dawns, chill from his rippling rest, the seagull’s wings shall dip and pivot him?” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem which asks its title object to “Unto us lowliest sometime sweep, descend, and of the curveship lend a myth to God.”
The Bridge is the magnum opus of this modernist poet, who committed suicide at thirty-two by jumping off a steam ship into the Gulf of Mexico.

The Bridge is a tribute to the title landmark between Manhattan and this borough on Long Island. That neo-gothic bridge named for this borough crosses the East River, and many who worked on constructing its foundations came down with the bends.

bonuses.leadin: In this poem, its author seemed to prophesy his own death with lines like “I must first be lost in fatal tides”. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem inspired by Emil Oppfer, which opens by describing how “above the fresh ruffles of the surf, bright striped urchins flay each other with sand” and ends “it is the unbetrayable reply/whose accent no farewell can know”.
Voyages is a poem by this American poet, who attempted to outdo TS Eliot’s The Waste Land in his own masterpiece, The Bridge.
The Bridge is titled in honour of a real-life bridge, which connects Manhattan to this most populous borough of New York. Walt Whitman described “crossing” this area’s “ferry” in an earlier poem.
answers: Voyages [accept specific parts of the poem, I-VI, as long as Voyages is mentioned]

Hart Crane (prompt on Crane; do not accept Stephen Crane)

bonuses.leadin: These vehicles stir up so much dust that they hide the Xianyang (shee-ahn-yahng) Bridge in a poem lamenting that male children “are merely buried amid the grass.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name these vehicles that “rumble and roll” on their way to war in a Tang dynasty poem titled for their “song” or “ballad” and written by Du Fu.
This contemporary of Du Fu was a Tang dynasty poet who wrote “The River Merchant’s Wife,” which was adapted into an English poem by Ezra Pound.

Several Tang dynasty poems feature the “Yellow Crane,” a structure of this type that is mentioned in the title of a Li Po poem that describes “seeing off Meng Haoran.” An English-language poem titled for one of these structures begins by describing “the bell-rope that gathers God at dawn.”
answers: army wagons [or army carts; or chariots; accept “Song of the Wagons” or “Ballad of the Wagons” or “Ballad of the Army Carts” or “Song of the Chariots” or “Bing Che Xing”]
towers [accept Yellow Crane Tower or Huáng hè Lóu or “The Broken Tower” (by Hart Crane)]

Li Po [or Li Bai; or Li Bo]
towers [accept Yellow Crane Tower or Huang he Lou or "The Broken Tower" (by Hart Crane)]

bonuses.leadin: The speaker of this poem describes bells that “swing I know not where” whose “tongues engrave / Membrane through marrow,” while wandering across a cathedral lawn. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem whose speaker enters the “world / to trace the visionary company of love” and eventually finds rejuvenation in “she / Whose sweet mortality stirs latent power.”
“The Broken Tower” is included in this poet’s collection White Buildings. This author of “Chaplinesque” also wrote an epic poem titled The Bridge.
Crane also wrote this series of poems about travels between “adagios of islands.” where the speaker asks “minstrel galleons of Carib fire” to “Bequeath us to no earthly shore.”

bonuses.leadin: In this poem’s opening portion, the speaker describes thinking “of cinemas, panoramic sleights/with multitudes bent toward some flashing scene.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this long poem, opening with a “proem” on the title structure, as well as sections titled “Cape Hatteras” and “Powhatan’s Daughter,” the latter of which includes the jazz-inspired “The River” about a journey west.

The Bridge is an epic poem by this American author, who included “The Broken Tower” in his collection White Buildings. In fine poetic tradition, this man committed suicide at 32 by jumping from a boat in the Gulf of Mexico.
Hart Crane also wrote a poem addressed “To” this reculsive American author, whose own works include the poems “Because I could not stop for death” and “I heard a fly buzz when I died.”

bonuses.leadin: NOTE TO MODERATORS: Do not read alternative answers. One poet with this surname wrote about “the dice of drowned men’s bones … bequeath[ing] an embassy” by washing up on the shore in one poem. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this surname, which is also shared by a poet who wrote a poem in which a bloody struggle ensues when a man says “Range me all the men of the world in rows.”
This Crane wrote about a “portent wound in corridors of shells” in “At Melville’s Tomb,” but is perhaps better known for penning The Bridge.
This collection by Stephen Crane includes a poem about a “creature, naked, bestial” that eats its own heart “because it is bitter / and because it is my heart.”
answers_sanitized: Crane [accept Hart Crane; accept Stephen Crane]
The Black Riders and Other Lines

bonuses.leadin: It begins with the claim “There are no stars tonight / But those of memory.” FTPE:
parts: Name this poem in which the speaker asks himself whether the “silence [is] strong enough / To carry back the music to its source / And back to you again” after discovering the title objects pressed in the corner of the roof, which belonged to “my mother’s mother / Elizabeth.”
This American poet included “My Grandmother Love Letters” in his first collection White Buildings before he published his epic poem The Bridge in 1930.

Crane also included this series of six poems written for the sailor Emil Opffer in White Buildings. Its most famous member describes how the Sea’s “undinal vast belly moonward bends, / Laughing the wrapt inflections of our love.”
